turns.content: okay I am losing my mind a little. been staring at this pytorch dataloader issue for like two hours and I feel like I'm going in circles
Two hours on a DataLoader bug sounds like exactly the kind of thing that needs fresh eyes. What's happening — error messages, slow performance, something weirder?

so it's a combination of things which is what's making it hard. the main symptom is my training loop just... stalls. like the GPU utilization drops to basically nothing between batches. I have num_workers=8 and it feels like it's doing nothing. I'm loading a pretty big multimodal dataset, like image + text pairs, and doing some preprocessing on the fly

Okay, GPU starving between batches with num_workers=8 and on-the-fly preprocessing — that's a classic DataLoader bottleneck pattern and there are a few likely culprits. First question: are you on Linux or are you running this on Windows or in a container? Because on Windows, DataLoader multiprocessing uses spawn instead of fork, which has significantly higher overhead per worker and can actually make things slower than num_workers=0 in some setups. If you're on Linux that's less likely the issue. Second: what does your preprocessing look like — are you doing anything compute-heavy in __getitem__, like decoding high-res images, tokenizing long text, doing augmentations? The bottleneck is often not the number of workers but what each worker is doing.

linux, so that's fine. and yeah the preprocessing is not trivial — I'm doing image resizing and normalization, and on the text side I'm running a tokenizer on the fly. the tokenizer is the one I'm suspicious of actually, it's a HuggingFace tokenizer and I don't know if those play nice with multiprocessing

Your suspicion about the HuggingFace tokenizer is well-founded — this is actually a known issue. HF fast tokenizers (the ones backed by the Rust tokenizers library) use internal parallelism via Rayon, and when you have multiple DataLoader workers each spinning up their own tokenizer, you get a thread explosion: 8 workers × however many threads the tokenizer wants = chaos, with a lot of time spent on thread contention rather than actual work. The fix is to set this environment variable before spawning your DataLoader workers:

```python
import os
os.environ["TOKENIZERS_PARALLELISM"] = "false"
```

Set that at the top of your script before any imports of tokenizers. This disables the Rust-level parallelism inside each tokenizer instance, so your DataLoader workers can actually run in parallel without fighting each other. This alone often produces a dramatic speedup in exactly the symptom you're describing. Have you tried that yet, or is this new information?
oh my god I have literally seen that warning in my logs and dismissed it every time. "huggingface/tokenizers: The current process just got forked..." and I always just scroll past it

Ha — yeah, that warning is extremely easy to scroll past and it's basically screaming at you that this exact thing is happening. That's almost certainly your primary bottleneck. Set TOKENIZERS_PARALLELISM=false and rerun, you should see a big difference in GPU utilization immediately. While you're at it, a few other things worth checking in parallel: make sure you have pin_memory=True in your DataLoader if you're moving data to CUDA — it pre-pins the memory for faster host-to-device transfer. Also set prefetch_factor=2 or 4 (it's the default in newer PyTorch but worth being explicit). And if your images are stored as high-res PNGs or TIFFs, consider pre-converting them to JPEG or WebP at a reasonable resolution, or better yet converting your dataset to a format like WebDataset or LMDB so you're doing sequential reads instead of random filesystem I/O for each sample — random I/O at scale is a silent killer.

pin_memory I already have. prefetch_factor I don't think I set explicitly. the images are JPEGs thankfully, so that's fine. but the LMDB thing is interesting — I've heard of it but never used it. is it a big lift to convert?

Not a huge lift for the conversion itself — you basically iterate your dataset once, write each sample into LMDB, and then your __getitem__ just does a key lookup instead of an os.open. The main thing to know: LMDB is memory-mapped, so reads are extremely fast and play very nicely with multiprocessing (no file handle contention). The rough pattern looks like this:

```python
import lmdb
import pickle

# Writing
env = lmdb.open('my_dataset.lmdb', map_size=1e12)  # set map_size generously
with env.begin(write=True) as txn:
    for idx, (image_bytes, text) in enumerate(your_raw_data):
        sample = {'image': image_bytes, 'text': text}
        txn.put(str(idx).encode(), pickle.dumps(sample))

# Reading in __getitem__
env = lmdb.open('my_dataset.lmdb', readonly=True, lock=False)
with env.begin() as txn:
    sample = pickle.loads(txn.get(str(idx).encode()))
```

The lock=False on read is important for multiprocessing. Honestly though — fix the tokenizer parallelism issue first. That's probably 80% of your problem. LMDB is worth it at scale but don't optimize prematurely if the tokenizer fix gets you where you need to be.
okay trying the tokenizer fix right now... running a quick training loop to check GPU util
Good, watch nvidia-smi in another terminal — you should see the GPU utilization stop doing that dipping-to-zero pattern between batches. Fingers crossed.
OKAY. okay wow. GPU util is like... consistently in the 80-90% range now. before it was spiking and dropping, spiking and dropping. that one env variable. I'm a little embarrassed lol

Don't be embarrassed — that warning is notoriously easy to ignore and the connection between "tokenizer forking warning" and "GPU starving" is not at all obvious. This is genuinely one of those bugs that trips up experienced ML engineers all the time. 80-90% GPU utilization is a healthy number. If you want to squeeze more out of it, prefetch_factor=4 might give you another small bump, and if you ever scale this up to a multi-node setup the LMDB approach will pay dividends. But honestly — great fix, and good instinct to be suspicious of the tokenizer in the first place.
I'm setting a reminder to never scroll past that warning again lol. okay this was incredibly useful, I was going to start randomly decreasing num_workers which would have made things worse

Yep, reducing num_workers would have "fixed" it by just not spawning the conflicting threads, which is technically a solution in the same way that unplugging a lamp fixes a flickering bulb. The real problem was always the parallelism conflict. Go enjoy your 80-90% GPU utilization — well earned.
